Definition: Also known as cherry-picking. The visualization selects partial truth and hides inconvenient elements, such as picking a specific time period, specific comparison opponents, or a particular set of data points.
Arises when connecting two dots with a straight line to form a line chart, creating a false impression of a trend.

A continuous variable is unnecessarily grouped into categories without purpose, making values near the boundaries of splits appear prominently different. For example, applying categorical rainbow colors to continuous variables exaggerates boundary differences.
Shows meaningless comparisons when numbers are in absolute values instead of being normalized, e.g. per capita. Common in population maps.
Hides patterns or shows misleading trends by putting items in an arbitrary order.
A regression line on weakly correlated data that misleads people into thinking there is a trend.
Can hide a recent declining trend by showing the accumulated historical values.
The y-axis does not start at zero, exaggerating differences between values by cutting off the lower portion of the axis.
Two data series with different magnitudes are plotted on two different y-axes, becoming misleading if the reader is unaware of the separate scales.
The axis direction is reversed from convention. Conventionally axes develop from smaller to larger numbers moving upward, but this is violated without clear justification.
A logarithmic scale is applied when a linear scale would be more appropriate (or expected), misleading readers unfamiliar with log scales.
The axis is unnecessarily extended beyond the data range, understating differences between values.
Two data series with very different magnitudes are plotted on the same axis, causing the smaller magnitude series to be dominated and barely visible.
Putting categorical data on axes or swapping the x and y-axis, applying a line chart to unsuitable data types.
Plotting a pie chart on data that has no part-to-whole relationship, misrepresenting how values relate to a whole.
Too many layers are stacked, making the chart incomprehensible for the reader.
Bar charts are applied in contexts where readers cannot properly comprehend the encoding.
Scatterplots are applied in contexts where readers cannot properly comprehend the encoding.
Too many colors are used in a visualization, overwhelming the reader and making the visualization hideous and hard to read.
The same or very similar colors are used for different categories, causing readers to mistake one category for another.
Colors used in the visualization are indistinguishable for color blind people, similar in effect to indistinguishable colors.
A visualization without a title, leaving the reader wondering what the visualization is about and what message it intends to convey.
The axis title is absent, making the meaning of the axis ambiguous.
The legend is absent; can sometimes be compensated for with labels on the visual marks, but when all labeling is absent the visualization is incomprehensible.
Value labels are absent from the chart, leaving readers unable to determine exact values.
The axis itself is absent from the visualization.
The tick marks on the axis are absent.
The units of measurement are not displayed on the chart.
Data values are drawn disproportionately or not to scale. Commonly spotted as value labels that fail to match the visually encoded geometric objects.
Tick intervals in the chart are not consistent, violating the reader's expectation and showing false patterns. Can be exploited maliciously to create a distorted visual perception.
The boundaries of binning groups vary, putting more or fewer items into specific groups and resulting in manipulated statistical calculations.
A color mismatch between colors and the categories they represent.
Some entities are grouped while others are not, making a dominating entity look less dominant by splitting it into smaller members while other groups remain whole.
Tick labels are not in the same format across the axis.
Value labels are inconsistently annotated across the chart.
A data value is plotted beyond the axis range so that its geometric mark falls outside the chart area.
Text that overlaps itself or other text and becomes unreadable.
Text that is too small in proportion to the chart such that it is hardly or not readable for people
A perspective distortion technique where closer objects appear larger despite being the same size, allowing more visual ink for smaller objects in 3D bar charts.
Data values are linearly encoded as areas, but according to Stevens' power law (exponent ~0.7 for area) readers consistently underestimate the values.

Use of rainbow colors, categorical colors on sequential data, or sequential colors on categorical data. Dichotomy colors can hide underlying distributions (e.g., a slight majority conceals a near-equal minority).
Interpolation between data points creates non-existing data points, misleading readers about the actual data.
The aspect ratio of the chart is distorted, commonly seen in line charts, affecting the perceived slope and trends.
The title does not match the actual message conveyed by the chart.
Visual marks overlap in the same position, hiding overlapping marks from the reader and resulting in understated areas or densities.
